content.text: Context length — also called context window or context size — is the maximum number of tokens a language model can process in a single forward pass. Every token in the window participates in the attention computation, meaning the model can directly reference any earlier token without external memory. This is fundamentally different from RNNs, which compress history into a fixed-size hidden state and suffer from vanishing gradients over long sequences.

A token is roughly 3–4 characters of English text. One page of text ≈ 500 words ≈ 650–700 tokens. So a 128K-token context window holds roughly 180 pages of text — an entire novella or a long legal contract. The practical implication is profound: tasks that once required retrieval pipelines (chunking documents, embedding, similarity search) can sometimes be replaced by simply placing the entire document in the context window.

Context length is a hard architectural limit set at training time by the maximum sequence length the model was trained on and the position encoding scheme used. Exceeding this limit causes undefined behavior — most implementations truncate, but some wrap around or error. The limit cannot be extended post-hoc without fine-tuning or architectural modifications.

Short context limits force application designers to make lossy compromises. A 4K-token limit means a 50-page contract cannot fit in the window; the developer must chunk it, embed each chunk, retrieve the most relevant chunks, and hope the answer is not split across chunks. Every chunking decision is a potential information loss. Longer context windows eliminate entire categories of preprocessing complexity.

The stakes are highest in agentic and long-running tasks. An AI coding agent working on a large codebase needs to hold many files in context simultaneously to reason about cross-file dependencies. A customer support system handling a long conversation thread needs to reference earlier turns without losing context. A research assistant summarizing a 300-page report needs to reason over the full document, not just fragments.
Small-to-medium documents fit entirely in the window, removing the complexity of chunking, embedding, and similarity search.
The model can directly attend to any token — footnotes in a legal contract can reference clauses 50 pages earlier without preprocessing.
Long contexts allow dozens of in-context examples, dramatically improving accuracy on structured tasks without fine-tuning.
Customer support, tutoring, and coding assistants benefit from remembering the full conversation history without external summarization.
Most APIs charge per input token. A 200K-token context costs 100× more than 2K tokens in input fees, and takes proportionally longer.
The prefill phase (processing the input context before generation begins) scales as O(n²) in compute and O(n) in memory. At 128K tokens, prefill can take seconds even on high-end hardware.

Empirical research shows models retrieve information reliably from the beginning and end of the context but underperform on content in the middle. Long context does not guarantee uniform attention quality.
Autoregressive generation caches key/value tensors for all previous tokens. At 128K context for a 70B model, the KV cache alone requires 26+ GB of GPU memory.

Context length is the maximum number of tokens a model can hold in its attention computation at one time. Every token in the context window can attend to every other token — that is the definition of the transformer's full attention. This gives context length its power (any token can reference any earlier token directly) and its cost (attention is quadratic in sequence length). The field has been in a sustained race to push this limit: GPT-3 shipped with 2,048 tokens in 2020; by 2025, production models routinely support 128K–200K tokens, with research systems exceeding 1 million.
Why Context Length Was Hard to Scale
Standard scaled dot-product attention has time and memory complexity
attention matrix. Doubling the context length quadruples the attention memory and roughly quadruples the compute:
matrix fits comfortably in GPU HBM. At 128K tokens, that matrix would be 128K × 128K × 4 bytes = 64 GB per head — impossibly large. The practical breakthrough was
(Dao et al., 2022): it computes exact attention without ever materialising the full
matrix by tiling the computation across SRAM blocks. Time complexity stays
but memory for the attention matrix drops to
Context Length Growth in Production Models

Extending context length requires the model to handle position indices it never saw during training. Learned absolute position embeddings (GPT-2) have a fixed maximum length and cannot extrapolate. Modern approaches embed relative position information directly into the attention computation, enabling length generalisation:

import torch
import math


def precompute_rope_freqs(dim: int, max_seq_len: int, base: float = 10_000.0) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) — used by LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX.
    Returns a (max_seq_len, dim/2) complex frequency tensor.

    Key property: the dot product Q_m · K_n depends only on (m - n),
    the relative distance between positions m and n, not absolute indices.
    This lets the model extrapolate to longer sequences than it saw in training.
    """
    inv_freq = 1.0 / (base ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2).float() / dim))
    t = torch.arange(max_seq_len, dtype=torch.float32)
    freqs = torch.outer(t, inv_freq)            # (seq_len, dim/2)
    return torch.polar(torch.ones_like(freqs), freqs)  # complex: e^(i·freqs)


def apply_rope(x: torch.Tensor, freqs: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Apply RoPE to a query or key tensor of shape (B, seq_len, n_heads, head_dim).
    """
    x_complex = torch.view_as_complex(x.float().reshape(*x.shape[:-1], -1, 2))
    freqs = freqs[: x.shape[1]].unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(2)  # (1, seq, 1, dim/2)
    x_rotated = x_complex * freqs
    return torch.view_as_real(x_rotated).reshape(x.shape).to(x.dtype)


# RoPE scaling for context extension (YaRN / rope_scaling in HuggingFace):
# Increase `base` to spread frequencies and handle longer distances.
# LLaMA 3 uses base=500_000 (vs original 10_000) to support 128K context.
def scale_rope_base(original_base: float, original_ctx: int, target_ctx: int) -> float:
    """Approximate base scaling for context extension."""
    scale_factor = target_ctx / original_ctx
    return original_base * (scale_factor ** (original_base / (original_base - 1)))

new_base = scale_rope_base(10_000, 4096, 128_000)
print(f"Scaled RoPE base: {new_base:,.0f}")  # ~≈470,000
KV Cache Memory at Different Context Lengths

During autoregressive generation, the key and value tensors for all previous tokens are cached (the KV cache) to avoid recomputing them on every step. At long contexts this cache dominates GPU memory:

def kv_cache_bytes(
    seq_len: int,
    n_layers: int,
    n_kv_heads: int,
    head_dim: int,
    dtype_bytes: int = 2,   # BF16 = 2 bytes
) -> int:
    """
    KV cache size in bytes for a single sequence.
    There are 2 tensors per layer (K and V), each of shape
    (seq_len, n_kv_heads, head_dim).
    """
    return 2 * n_layers * seq_len * n_kv_heads * head_dim * dtype_bytes


# LLaMA 3 8B config: 32 layers, 8 KV heads (GQA), head_dim=128
configs = {
    "LLaMA 3 8B": dict(n_layers=32, n_kv_heads=8,  head_dim=128),
    "LLaMA 3 70B": dict(n_layers=80, n_kv_heads=8,  head_dim=128),
    "Mistral 7B":  dict(n_layers=32, n_kv_heads=8,  head_dim=128),
}

seq_lens = [4_096, 32_768, 128_000]
print(f"{'Model':15s}  " + "  ".join(f"{s//1024:>7}K" for s in seq_lens))
print("-" * 55)
for name, cfg in configs.items():
    sizes = [kv_cache_bytes(s, **cfg) / 1e9 for s in seq_lens]
    print(f"{name:15s}  " + "  ".join(f"{sz:>7.2f}GB" for sz in sizes))
# LLaMA 3 8B      0.27GB    2.15GB    8.39GB
# LLaMA 3 70B     0.84GB    6.71GB   26.21GB
# Mistral 7B       0.27GB    2.15GB    8.39GB
#
# A 128K-context LLaMA 3 70B generation needs >26 GB just for the KV cache,
# on top of ~140 GB for the model weights (BF16).
Empirical research (Liu et al., 2023) showed that transformer models tend to retrieve information reliably from the

of their context window, but struggle with content placed deep in the middle. This is a U-shaped performance curve across positions. The practical implication is that simply stuffing a long context with relevant documents does not guarantee the model will use all of them equally — placement matters. Place the most important instructions and facts near the start (system prompt) or the very end (immediately before the model's turn).

A long context window is a capability, not a strategy. Having 200K tokens available does not mean retrieval quality is uniform across all 200K positions, that attending over 200K tokens is free, or that stuffing the window is better than retrieval-augmented generation. Think of the context window as expensive working memory: put in what the model genuinely needs for this specific call, and cache or retrieve the rest.
When to Use Long Context vs. RAG
Long context and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) solve overlapping problems. The choice depends on document size, latency budget, cost sensitivity, and the nature of reasoning required.
Larger than context window or dynamic corpus
Lower token cost, but embedding/retrieval infra cost
Lower model latency, add retrieval RTT
Provided at call time — always fresh
Using long context effectively requires understanding token budgets, placement strategy, and when to fall back to RAG. The following examples show common patterns.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def count_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    """Approximate token count: ~4 chars per token for English."""
    return len(text) // 4

def summarize_document(document_text: str, question: str) -> str:
    """
    Strategy: put the document before the question.
    This takes advantage of the 'recency effect' — the question
    at the very end gets strong attention from the model.
    """
    token_estimate = count_tokens(document_text)
    print(f"Document ~{token_estimate:,} tokens")

    if token_estimate > 190_000:
        raise ValueError("Document too large for 200K context — use RAG instead")

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system="You are a document analyst. Answer questions based only on the provided document.",
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"<document>\n{document_text}\n</document>\n\n{question}"
            }
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text


def multi_document_analysis(docs: list[str], task: str) -> str:
    """
    Pattern: XML-tagged documents in context.
    Clear delimiters help the model distinguish documents.
    """
    tagged = "\n".join(
        f'<document id="{i+1}">\n{doc}\n</document>' for i, doc in enumerate(docs)
    )

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"{tagged}\n\n{task}"
            }
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text


# Prompt caching: freeze the long document prefix across multiple queries
# Use cache_control to cache the document tokens (reduces cost 90%+ on repeat queries)
def cached_document_qa(document_text: str, questions: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """
    Use Anthropic prompt caching to serve multiple questions
    against the same document without re-processing tokens each time.
    """
    answers = []
    for q in questions:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=[
                {
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"<document>\n{document_text}\n</document>\nAnswer questions about this document concisely.",
                    "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}  # cache this prefix
                }
            ],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": q}],
        )
        answers.append(response.content[0].text)
    return answers

A legal technology firm processes Master Service Agreements (MSAs) that average 80–120 pages (60K–90K tokens). With a 200K context window, the entire contract fits without chunking. The system must identify non-standard clauses, flag liability caps below $1M, and summarize indemnification terms across multiple sections that cross-reference each other.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LEGAL_REVIEW_SYSTEM = """You are a legal contract analyst specializing in MSAs.
Review contracts for:
1. Liability caps below $1M USD — flag as HIGH risk
2. Unlimited liability provisions — flag as CRITICAL
3. Broad indemnification language covering third-party IP — flag as MEDIUM risk
4. Auto-renewal clauses without adequate notice periods — flag as LOW risk

Return findings as JSON: {"findings": [{"clause": "...", "risk": "...", "excerpt": "..."}]}
"""

def review_contract(contract_text: str) -> dict:
    """
    Review a full contract in a single long-context call.
    The model can reason across the entire document simultaneously,
    catching cross-references between sections that RAG-based chunking would miss.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=4096,
        system=LEGAL_REVIEW_SYSTEM,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"<contract>\n{contract_text}\n</contract>\n\n"
                    "Review this contract and return findings as JSON."
                )
            }
        ],
    )

    raw = response.content[0].text.strip()
    # Strip markdown code fences if present
    if raw.startswith("```"):
        raw = raw.split("\n", 1)[1].rsplit("\n", 1)[0]
    return json.loads(raw)

# Usage:
# with open("msa_contract.txt") as f:
#     contract = f.read()
# findings = review_contract(contract)
# print(f"Found {len(findings['findings'])} risk items")
print("Legal contract review function defined.")
print("Key advantage: cross-section reasoning impossible with chunked RAG.")
Adding every potentially relevant document to the context regardless of actual relevance. This wastes tokens, increases cost, and can dilute the model's focus.
Placing critical instructions in the middle.
The lost-in-the-middle effect is real. Format constraints, critical rules, and the question itself should be placed at the start or end of the context.
Benchmarking only generation speed and ignoring the prefill phase. A 128K-token context can take 5–15 seconds to prefill even on powerful hardware — this dominates latency for most use cases.
Even with the answer in the context, models can hallucinate if the question is ambiguous or the relevant passage is placed poorly. Long context reduces but does not eliminate hallucination.
Not using prompt caching for repeated contexts.
Sending the same large document multiple times wastes tokens and budget. APIs like Anthropic support prompt caching to amortize input processing cost across multiple queries.
Put the most important instructions at the beginning and end.
System prompt → documents (XML-tagged) → task instructions immediately before the model's turn.
Wrap documents in XML tags (<document id="1">) to help the model distinguish sources and reduce confusion when citing.
If the same document is queried repeatedly, use prompt caching. Anthropic charges ~10% of normal token price for cache reads, reducing cost by 90%.
Measure actual retrieval quality, not just BLEU/ROUGE.
For multi-hop reasoning tasks, evaluate whether the model actually uses information from different parts of the context.

For very large corpora, use RAG to retrieve the top-K relevant chunks, then place those chunks in a long-context call for synthesis. Combines precision of retrieval with reasoning power of full-context attention.
Context length is the number of tokens a model can attend to simultaneously; it is a hard architectural limit set at training time.
Standard attention is O(n²) in memory; FlashAttention reduced memory to O(n) by tiling, enabling 128K+ contexts on real hardware.
RoPE and other relative position encodings enable length generalization; learned absolute embeddings cannot extrapolate beyond their training length.
The KV cache grows as O(n) per layer per head; at 128K context a 70B model needs 26 GB just for the cache.
The lost-in-the-middle effect means information at the beginning and end of context is retrieved more reliably than information in the middle.
Long context and RAG are complementary: use long context for full-document reasoning, RAG for large corpora where only a fraction of the data is relevant.
Prompt caching reduces the cost of repeated long-context queries by ~90% by reusing processed prefix tokens.
A long context window is expensive working memory — put only what the model genuinely needs for the specific call.
